explaining and declaring that it was necessary for Christ to suffer and <sup>[then]</sup> rise again from the dead. Paul was saying, “This Jesus, whom I am proclaiming to you, is the Christ.”

Some of the Jews were convinced <sup>[that Jesus was the Messiah]</sup> and so joined with Paul and Silas. Also, a large number of devoted Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup> and leading women <sup>[joined their group]</sup>.

But <sup>[other]</sup> Jews became jealous and, recruiting certain ungodly riffraff, they gathered a mob and brought the city to near-riot conditions. They <sup>[even]</sup> attacked Jason's house and attempted to bring Paul and Silas before the <sup>[assembly of]</sup> people.

When they could not find them, they dragged Jason and some other brothers before the city officials and shouted, “These men have turned the world upside down <sup>[i.e., disrupted society by their preaching]</sup> and have <sup>[now]</sup> come here also <sup>[i.e., to do the same thing in our community]</sup>.

Jason has welcomed them and <sup>[now]</sup> all of them <sup>[i.e., Paul, Silas, Jason and the others]</sup> are going against the <sup>[Roman]</sup> laws of Caesar by saying that someone else is <sup>[our]</sup> king; that Jesus is <sup>[king]</sup>!”
When they heard <sup>[these charges]</sup>, the crowds and <sup>[even]</sup> the city officials became very disturbed <sup>[by the situation stirred up by the Jews]</sup>.
They made Jason and the others post bail, and then released them.
The brothers <sup>[then]</sup> immediately sent Paul and Silas away at night to Berea <sup>[i.e., a town in Macedonia]</sup>. When they got there they went into the Jewish synagogue.

Now these people <sup>[in Berea]</sup> had more character than those in Thessalonica because they <sup>[not only]</sup> received the message with an open mind but <sup>[also]</sup> examined the <sup>[Old Testament]</sup> Scriptures every day to see if what they had heard was really true.
Many people therefore became believers <sup>[in Christ]</sup>, including a number of leading Greek women and also a number of men.
But when the Jews of Thessalonica learned that Paul was proclaiming the message of God at Berea also, they went there too, and incited and upset the crowds.
So, immediately the brothers sent Paul clear over to the coast, while Silas and Timothy remained there <sup>[at Berea]</sup>.
But those who escorted Paul took him to Athens <sup>[i.e., in Greece]</sup> and, after being directed to have Silas and Timothy join him there as soon as possible, they left.
Now while Paul was waiting for them <sup>[to arrive]</sup> in Athens, he was deeply stirred in his spirit when he saw the city so full of idols.
So, he debated in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing people <sup>[i.e., Gentile proselytes]</sup>, as well as in the open shopping market with others who met with him there.

Also certain Epicurean and Stoic philosophers approached him for discussion. Some asked, “What will this know-it-all have to say?” Others said, “He seems to be advocating <sup>[a belief in]</sup> some different gods.” <sup>[They said this]</sup> because he was proclaiming Jesus and the resurrection <sup>[of the dead]</sup>.
So, they led him to the Areopagus <sup>[i.e., an elevated assembly place called “the Hill of Mars”]</sup> and asked him, “Could we learn <sup>[more]</sup> about this new teaching you are speaking of?
You are telling us some very unusual things and we would like to know what they mean.”
(Now the local people of Athens, along with foreigners who lived there, spent <sup>[about]</sup> all their time telling about or listening to some new idea.)
Then Paul stood up in the Areopagus and said, “You men of Athens, I noticed that you are an extremely religious people in all your ways. <sup>[See verse 16]</sup>

For as I traveled along <sup>[your streets]</sup> and observed what you are worshiping, I saw an altar with the inscription: <sup>[DEDICATED]</sup> TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. So, what you are worshiping ignorantly is what I am telling you about.
The God who made the world and everything in it, who is Lord of heaven and earth, does not live in hand-made temples.
Neither is He waited on by men’s hands as though He needed anything, since it is He who gives to all people their life and breath and everything <sup>[they need]</sup>.
He made every race of people living throughout the earth from one <sup>[family]</sup> and determined when <sup>[they would rise in history]</sup> and where they would live.
He wanted these people to search for God in hope that, by groping for Him, they might <sup>[eventually]</sup> find Him, even though He is not <sup>[really]</sup> very far from <sup>[any of]</sup> us.

For in <sup>[the strength of]</sup> God we <sup>[all]</sup> live, move around and have our <sup>[personal]</sup> identity, just as a certain one of your <sup>[Athenian]</sup> poets <sup>[once]</sup> said, ‘For we too are His children.’
Since therefore we are God’s children, we should not think that the Deity is like gold, silver or stone, to be carved <sup>[into an idol]</sup> by the design and skill of men.

Now God made allowance for the times when people were still uninformed <sup>[about His complete will]</sup>, but now <sup>[in the Gospel age]</sup> He requires all people everywhere to repent <sup>[i.e., change their hearts and lives]</sup>.

For He has appointed a <sup>[certain]</sup> day when He will judge the people of the world according to <sup>[a standard of]</sup> true justice by the man <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> whom He has appointed <sup>[as Judge, See II Tim. 4:8]</sup>. <sup>[And]</sup> He has given assurance to all people <sup>[that He will do this]</sup> by raising Jesus from the dead.”
Now when the people heard about the resurrection from the dead, some of them made fun of it, but others said, “We would like to hear <sup>[more]</sup> from you about this again.”
So, Paul left <sup>[the Areopagus]</sup>.
But certain men continued to listen to him and became believers <sup>[in Jesus]</sup>. Among them was Dionysius, <sup>[an official]</sup> of the Areopagus, a woman named Damaris and others with them.
After this happened Paul left Athens and went to Corinth <sup>[i.e., a principal city of Greece]</sup>.

There he met a certain Jewish man named Aquila, a native of Pontus, <sup>[in northern Asia Minor]</sup> who, with his wife Priscilla, had recently come from Rome, because Claudius <sup>[the Roman Emperor]</sup> had ordered all Jews out of that city. Paul met this couple
and, because they followed the same trade of tent making, he stayed with them and went to work <sup>[for them]</sup>.
Every Sabbath day Paul held discussions in the synagogues, trying to convince <sup>[both]</sup> Jews and Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles, that Jesus was the Messiah]</sup>.
But when Silas and Timothy arrived from Macedonia <sup>[i.e., from the town of Berea]</sup>, Paul began devoting his full time to declaring the message to Jews that Jesus was the Christ.

When the Jews resisted <sup>[Paul’s efforts]</sup> and spoke against him and his message, he shook out his clothing <sup>[i.e., an expression of rejection and contempt]</sup> and said to them “Let your blood be on your own heads <sup>[i.e., you are responsible for whatever harm comes from your action]</sup>; I am not responsible. From now on I will go <sup>[and preach]</sup> to the Gentiles <sup>[only]</sup>.”
So, he left <sup>[this assembly of Jews]</sup> and went to the house of a certain man named Titius Justus, a worshiper of God, whose house was right next door to the synagogue.

Crispus, the leader of the synagogue, and all of his family became believers in the Lord. <sup>[And]</sup> many of the Corinthians who heard <sup>[Paul’s message]</sup> believed <sup>[in the Lord]</sup> and were immersed <sup>[into Christ]</sup>.
Then the Lord spoke to Paul in a night vision <sup>[i.e., a divinely given dream]</sup>: “Do not be afraid, but speak up and do not hold <sup>[anything]</sup> back,
for I will be with you <sup>[See Matt. 28:20]</sup> and no one will attack you or hurt you, for I have many people in this city <sup>[i.e., who will be converted]</sup>.”
So, Paul lived there <sup>[in Corinth]</sup> for eighteen months, teaching God’s message among the inhabitants <sup>[of the city]</sup>.
But when Gallio was magistrate of Achaia <sup>[i.e., the southern province of Greece]</sup> the Jews joined forces to attack Paul and brought him before the court of justice,
and said <sup>[about him]</sup>, “This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the Law <sup>[of Moses]</sup>.”

When Paul was about to respond <sup>[to this charge]</sup>, Gallio said to the Jews, “<sup>[My]</sup> Jewish <sup>[constituents]</sup>, if it were a simple case of wrongdoing or some serious crime, there would be good reason for me to consider your charge <sup>[against this man]</sup>.
But if it is only a dispute over words, titles and your <sup>[religious]</sup> law, tend to it yourselves; I refuse to pass judgment on such <sup>[trivial]</sup> matters.”
And he had them thrown out of the courtroom.

Then, all of them <sup>[Note: This “all” could refer to the Greeks, the Jews or the Roman officers. Since the text does not specify, it seems most reasonable to suggest it was the Romans]</sup> grabbed Sosthenes, the leader of the synagogue, and beat him in front of the court of justice. But Gallio showed no interest in the whole affair.

After this <sup>[incident]</sup> Paul remained <sup>[in Corinth]</sup> for some time before leaving the brothers and sailing for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila. Paul shaved his head while in Cenchrea as part of a vow he had taken.
When they arrived at Ephesus, Paul left Priscilla and Aquila there. He then went into the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> synagogue and held discussions with the Jews.
When they asked him to stay <sup>[and continue the discussions]</sup> longer, he declined.
So, he left them, saying, “I will come back to you if it is God’s will.” Then He sailed from Ephesus.

And when he landed at Ceasarea, he went up <sup>[Note: This would mean either up to Ceasarea or up to Jerusalem]</sup> and greeted the church <sup>[there]</sup>, then went down to Antioch <sup>[in Syria]</sup>.

After spending some time there, he left and traveled through the district of Galatia, <sup>[and]</sup> then through Phrygia, strengthening <sup>[spiritually]</sup> all the disciples <sup>[along the way]</sup>.
Now a certain Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria <sup>[Egypt]</sup> and an effective speaker, who was well-grounded in the <sup>[Old Testament]</sup> Scriptures, came to Ephesus.

He was a man who had learned the way of the Lord, and with spiritual fervor, taught accurately <sup>[what he knew]</sup> about Jesus, although he knew <sup>[and had received]</sup> only the immersion <sup>[taught and practiced]</sup> by John.

He began speaking boldly in the synagogue and when Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they invited him to their home and proceeded to explain to him God's way more accurately <sup>[than he had known]</sup>.

And when he desired to travel over into Achaia, he was encouraged <sup>[to do so]</sup> by the brothers, who wrote to the disciples <sup>[in Achaia and urged them]</sup> to welcome him. When he arrived <sup>[in Achaia]</sup> he was a great help to those who had become believers through the unearned favor of God. <sup>[Note: An alternate meaning is, “he was a great help, through God’s unearned favor upon him, to those who had become believers”]</sup>.
For Apollos effectively refuted <sup>[the position of]</sup> the Jews, publicly proving, by using the <sup>[Old Testament]</sup> Scriptures, that Jesus was the Christ.
Then, while Apollos was <sup>[still]</sup> at Corinth, Paul traveled through the <sup>[western]</sup> districts and came to Ephesus where he found some disciples.

He asked them, “Did <sup>[any of]</sup> you receive the <sup>[indwelling]</sup> Holy Spirit when you became believers <sup>[in Jesus]</sup>?” They answered him, “No, we have never <sup>[even]</sup> heard that there was a Holy Spirit.”
Paul <sup>[again]</sup> asked, “Into what were you immersed then?” They answered him, “Into John’s immersion.”
Paul <sup>[then]</sup> said, “John immersed people who <sup>[demonstrated their]</sup> repentance, telling them they must believe in Jesus, the One who would come after him.”
When they heard this they were immersed into the name of the Lord Jesus.

Then Paul placed his hands on them and <sup>[the supernatural power of]</sup> the Holy Spirit came on them and they <sup>[began to]</sup> speak in <sup>[other]</sup> languages and prophesy <sup>[i.e., speak God's message]</sup>.
The total number <sup>[of those immersed]</sup> was twelve men.
Paul went into the Jewish synagogue and spoke boldly for three months, discussing and convincing people about matters regarding the kingdom of God.

But some became stubborn and refused to obey <sup>[the message]</sup>, saying false things about “the Way” <sup>[Note: This was a term used to designate the early church]</sup> in front of the large crowds. So, Paul left <sup>[the synagogue]</sup> and took the disciples and began holding discussions every day at Tyrannus’ school <sup>[house]</sup>.
This continued for two years so that all the Jews and Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup> who lived in <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia heard the message of the Lord.
And God performed special miracles through the hands of Paul,
such as sick people, who were given hand towels or <sup>[leather]</sup> aprons that had touched Paul’s body, being healed from diseases and delivered from evil spirits.

But certain traveling Jews, who practiced exorcism, decided that they too would attempt to speak the name of the Lord over people dominated of evil spirits while saying, “I command you <sup>[evil spirits to come out]</sup> by <sup>[the authority of]</sup> Jesus whom Paul preaches.”
<sup>[Now]</sup> the seven sons of a Jewish leading priest named Sceva were doing this.
<sup>[On one occasion]</sup> the evil spirit replied, “I know Jesus and I know Paul, but who are you?”
The man with the evil spirit then jumped on all of them, took control, then overpowered them so that they ran out of the house with their clothes torn off and badly beaten.
When this incident became known to all of the Jews and Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup> who lived in Ephesus, they became fearful and the name of the Lord Jesus was regarded with honor.
Many of the believers also came confessing their sins and describing what they had <sup>[previously]</sup> done.

And a large number of them, who had been involved in occultic practices, brought their books <sup>[on the occult]</sup> and began burning them publicly. When they calculated the cost <sup>[of the books]</sup> it came to fifty thousand pieces of silver. <sup>[Note: The actual amount was approximately 150 years of a farm laborer’s pay, or more than 5 million dollars in 2005]</sup>.
So, the message of the Lord spread widely and had a great influence <sup>[over people]</sup>.
After these things happened Paul decided to travel through Macedonia and Achaia on his way to Jerusalem. He said, “Then, after I have been there I want to visit Rome also.”

After sending his two associates Timothy and Erastus on to Macedonia <sup>[Note: These were men who cared for various needs of his ministry]</sup>, Paul remained in <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia for a while.
About that time a large disturbance arose <sup>[in Ephesus]</sup> concerning “the Way.”

<sup>[Here is what happened]</sup>: A certain manufacturer of silver objects named Demetrius, who made silver replicas of the temple of Artemis <sup>[i.e., a Greek goddess]</sup>, brought much business to his workmen.
He called a meeting of his men and others involved in the same work and said to them, “Fellows, you know that we have made a good living from this work <sup>[of making temple replicas]</sup>.

And now you have seen and heard, not only here in Ephesus, but throughout almost the entire <sup>[province of]</sup> Asia, that this Paul has been convincing people, <sup>[even]</sup> to the point of turning many away <sup>[from idol worship]</sup>. <sup>[He is]</sup> saying that hand-made gods <sup>[like we make]</sup> are not real <sup>[gods at all]</sup>.

And not only is there a <sup>[real]</sup> danger that our trade will be discredited but also that the temple of our great goddess Artemis will be considered worthless and that even she <sup>[herself]</sup> will be dethroned from her magnificent place of being adored by all <sup>[in the province of]</sup> Asia and the world.”
When they heard this they became extremely angry and shouted out, “Great is <sup>[the goddess]</sup> Artemis, of the Ephesians.”
The city became full of confusion; they grabbed Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul’s Macedonian traveling companions, and everyone together rushed into the amphitheater.
And when Paul decided to go before the crowd <sup>[to talk with them]</sup>, the disciples would not allow him.
Some of his friends, who were officers in the province of Asia, sent word urging him not to take a chance by entering the amphitheater.
Some <sup>[in the crowd]</sup> were shouting one thing and some another; there was mass confusion, with most of the people not <sup>[even]</sup> knowing why they had assembled.

<sup>[It appears that]</sup> Alexander was appointed out of the crowd by the Jews <sup>[i.e., to be their spokesman in defending them from the charges being made against Paul]</sup>. He motioned with his hand <sup>[to get attention]</sup> and attempted to speak to the assembly in defense <sup>[of the Jewish viewpoint]</sup>.
But when the crowd realized that he was a Jew, they all joined together for about two hours shouting, “Great is <sup>[the goddess]</sup> Artemis, of the Ephesians.”

When the town clerk had quieted down the crowd, he said, “You people of Ephesus, who among you does not know that the city of Ephesus is caretaker of the temple of the great Artemis and of her image, which fell down from the sky?
Since no one can <sup>[really]</sup> say anything against these things, just calm down and do not do anything foolish.
For you have brought these men here --- <sup>[men]</sup> who have neither desecrated our temple nor defamed our goddess.

If therefore Demetrius, and the workmen associated with him, have a charge against anyone, the courts are open and there are magistrates <sup>[to judge such cases]</sup>; let them file their charges against each other.
But if you want to address other matters, they can be settled in the regular, scheduled assembly.

For we are truly in danger of being accused of inciting a riot here today when there is <sup>[really]</sup> no reason for it to happen. Then concerning this problem, we will not be able to explain how it all came about.”
And after the town clerk had said all this, he dismissed the assembly.
After the commotion died down, Paul sent for the <sup>[Ephesian]</sup> disciples and encouraged them before he left, <sup>[heading west]</sup> for Macedonia.
And when he had traveled through those districts and had delivered many messages of encouragement, he went on <sup>[south]</sup> into Greece,
where he spent three months. Then, just as he was about to set sail for Syria, Paul discovered that a plot was being laid against him by the Jews, so he decided to return through Macedonia.

The following men accompanied Paul as far as <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia <sup>[where Troas was located]</sup>: Sopater, the son of Pyrrhus, of Berea; Aristarchus and Secundus, of Thessalonica; Gaius, of Derbe; Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia; <sup>[seven in all]</sup>.

These <sup>[men]</sup> had gone ahead and were waiting for us <sup>[when we arrived]</sup> in Troas <sup>[i.e., a seaport town across the Aegean Sea from Philippi]</sup>. <sup>[Note: The use of “us” begins here again, suggesting that the writer Luke rejoins the party at Philippi. See next verse]</sup>.

And we <sup>[ourselves]</sup> sailed away from Philippi after the Festival of Unleavened Bread <sup>[Note: This was the Jewish feast commemorating deliverance from Egyptian bondage]</sup>, and five days later <sup>[we]</sup> joined them at Troas <sup>[i.e., the seven men mentioned in verses 4 and 5]</sup>, where we remained for seven days.

And on the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday]</sup>, when we <sup>[disciples]</sup> had gathered together to break bread <sup>[i.e., the Lord’s Supper. See I Cor. 11:20-24]</sup>, Paul delivered a message that lasted until midnight, <sup>[since]</sup> he was planning to leave <sup>[Troas]</sup> the next day.
Now there were many lamps in the upstairs room where we were meeting.

A certain young man, named Eutychus, fell sound asleep while sitting on an <sup>[open]</sup> window ledge <sup>[during the message]</sup>. Since Paul’s message continued <sup>[until nearly midnight]</sup> Eutychus was overcome by sleep and fell from the third floor to his death.
Paul hurried downstairs and placed himself on the young man and held him closely, saying <sup>[to the people gathered around]</sup>, “Do not be upset, he is alive.”
After that Paul went upstairs, broke bread and ate <sup>[a common meal]</sup>. <sup>[Following the meal]</sup> Paul talked with them for a long time, even until it got daylight, and then he left.
<sup>[In the meantime]</sup> they brought the young fellow <sup>[back upstairs]</sup> alive, and were greatly comforted.

But we went on ahead to the ship and sailed for Assos <sup>[i.e., a seaport town near Troas]</sup>, where we expected to take Paul on board. This is how Paul had planned it, intending to go there himself by land.
When he met us at Assos, we took him on board, then <sup>[together]</sup> we went to Mitylene <sup>[i.e., a town on a nearby island]</sup>.

And after leaving there, the next day we sailed past Chios <sup>[i.e., another small island on the way]</sup>, and the next day past Samos <sup>[i.e., another small island]</sup>, until <sup>[finally]</sup> on the following day we arrived at Miletus <sup>[i.e., another seaport town near Ephesus]</sup>.

For Paul had decided to sail on past Ephesus so he would not have to spend any time in <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia, because he was hurrying to get to Jerusalem in time for the Day of Pentecost, if at all possible.
And from Miletus Paul sent to Ephesus, requesting that the elders of the church <sup>[there]</sup> come to him.
And when they arrived, he said to them, “You <sup>[men]</sup> know the kind of life I lived when I was with you, from the <sup>[very]</sup> first day I set foot in <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia.
<sup>[You know that]</sup> I served the Lord with humility, with tears and with trials that I experienced as the result of plots by the Jews.
<sup>[You know]</sup> how I held back nothing in declaring to you whatever was profitable, and taught you in public <sup>[meetings]</sup> and in your homes.

<sup>[You know how]</sup> I testified to both Jews and Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup>, that they must repent <sup>[i.e., change their attitudes and behavior]</sup> toward God, and have faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ.
“And now, look, I am going to Jerusalem, impelled by the Holy Spirit, not knowing what will happen to me <sup>[when I get]</sup> there,
except that the Holy Spirit reveals to me in every city <sup>[I visit]</sup> that chains and persecutions await me there.

But I do not consider my life to be of value, as precious to me, <sup>[when compared to the importance of]</sup> accomplishing my task and the mission I received from the Lord Jesus to declare the good news of God’s unearned favor <sup>[through Christ]</sup>.
Now look, I know that none of you, among whom I traveled on preaching missions, will ever see my face again.
Therefore, I <sup>[must]</sup> declare to you today that I am not responsible for what happens to any of you.
For I held nothing back in declaring to you the entire message of God.

So, pay close attention to your <sup>[own]</sup> lives, and to all <sup>[members]</sup> of the flock <sup>[i.e., the congregation]</sup> of which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers <sup>[i.e., elders, shepherds or pastors]</sup>. Provide <sup>[spiritual]</sup> food for the church of the Lord which He purchased by <sup>[shedding]</sup> His own blood.

<sup>[Because]</sup> I know that after I am gone <sup>[from here]</sup> there will be vicious wolves <sup>[i.e., ravaging false teachers]</sup> who will come in among you, and they will not spare the flock <sup>[i.e., the congregation]</sup>.

<sup>[Indeed, even]</sup> from among your own selves <sup>[i.e., the body of elders there in Ephesus]</sup> some men will arise and, by speaking false things, they will lead away the disciples, <sup>[influencing them]</sup> to follow them.
So, be very watchful, and remember that for three years I never stopped warning every one of you <sup>[about such matters]</sup> both night and day with tears <sup>[in my eyes]</sup>.

“Now I commit you <sup>[elders]</sup> to God and to the message of His unearned favor which can build you up <sup>[spiritually]</sup> and provide you with the inheritance <sup>[of never ending life]</sup>, along with others who are <sup>[specially]</sup> set apart <sup>[for God]</sup>.
<sup>[You know that]</sup> I had no greedy desire for anyone’s silver, gold or clothing.
You <sup>[also]</sup> know that I worked with my own hands to provide not only my own needs, but the needs of those who were with me.

I have demonstrated before you a <sup>[good]</sup> example in all <sup>[these]</sup> things, so that you <sup>[too]</sup> will work hard to help those who are <sup>[physically]</sup> weak. <sup>[Also]</sup> remember the words of the Lord Jesus who Himself said, ‘It is a greater blessing <sup>[for you]</sup> to give <sup>[to people's needs]</sup> than to receive <sup>[help yourselves]</sup>.’”
After speaking these things Paul knelt down and prayed with all of the elders.
And when they all had a good cry, they embraced Paul and kissed him.
What saddened them most of all was when Paul said that they would never see his face again. Then they accompanied him to his ship.

After we had <sup>[reluctantly]</sup> left <sup>[the Ephesian elders]</sup> and set sail, we traveled straight <sup>[south]</sup> to Cos <sup>[i.e., a small island]</sup> and the next day to Rhodes <sup>[i.e., another island]</sup>, and from there on to Patara <sup>[i.e., a seaport town on the southern coast of the province of Asia Minor]</sup>.
Here we found a ship heading across <sup>[the open sea]</sup> for Phoenicia, boarded it and sailed away.

When we sighted Cyprus <sup>[i.e., a large island]</sup>, we sailed past it on our port side and arrived at Tyre <sup>[i.e., a major seaport]</sup> in Syria <sup>[i.e., on the west coast of Palestine]</sup>, where the ship was to unload its cargo.

When we located the disciples <sup>[there]</sup> we stayed <sup>[with them]</sup> for seven days. They advised Paul, through <sup>[inspired revelations from]</sup> the Holy Spirit, not <sup>[even]</sup> to set foot in Jerusalem.

When we had completed preparations for the next day’s voyage we left, and headed out <sup>[to sea]</sup> on our journey. All the disciples, together with their wives and children, escorted us out of the city and knelt down and prayed with us as we said goodbye to each other.
Then we went aboard the ship while the disciples returned home again.
When we completed our voyage from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais and greeted the brothers there, then stayed with them for a day.

The next day we left and came to Caesarea, where we went to the house of Philip, the evangelist, who had been one of the seven <sup>[“deacons” chosen by the Jerusalem church to minister to widows, See Acts 6]</sup>. We stayed with him <sup>[while there]</sup>.
This man had four virgin <sup>[i.e., unmarried]</sup> daughters, who were female prophets <sup>[i.e., they spoke by inspiration]</sup>.
As we waited there for a number of days, a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.

When he met with us, he took Paul's belt, tied his own hands and feet <sup>[with it]</sup>, and said, “The Holy Spirit has revealed <sup>[to me]</sup> that the man who owns this belt will be tied up like this by the Jews in Jerusalem and will be turned over to the Gentiles.”
When we heard these things, both we and those who lived there <sup>[i.e., Philip, his daughters and local disciples]</sup> urged Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.

<sup>[But]</sup> Paul answered, “What are you trying to do <sup>[by your]</sup> crying and breaking my heart <sup>[like this]</sup>? <sup>[Do you not know that]</sup> I am ready, not only to be chained up, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus?”
And when he could not be dissuaded <sup>[from going to Jerusalem]</sup> we stopped <sup>[trying to convince him]</sup>, saying, “Let the Lord's will be done in the matter.”
After these days we packed up our belongings and went up to Jerusalem.
Some of the disciples from Caesarea went with us also, bringing with them Mnason from Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we were to live <sup>[while in Jerusalem]</sup>.
And when we came to Jerusalem, the brothers <sup>[there]</sup> welcomed us gladly.
The next day Paul went with us to visit James <sup>[the Lord’s half-brother and a leader in the Jerusalem church]</sup>, with all of the elders <sup>[of the Jerusalem church]</sup> being present.
And after greeting them Paul reported one by one the things that God had brought about among the Gentiles through his ministry.

And when they heard <sup>[all]</sup> this, they gave honor to God, then said to Paul, “Brother, you can see how many thousands of Jewish believers <sup>[in Christ]</sup> there are <sup>[everywhere]</sup> and all of them are eager to observe the <sup>[requirements of the]</sup> Law of Moses.

Now these <sup>[Jewish Christians]</sup> have been informed that you are teaching all the Jews living among the Gentiles to give up <sup>[following the teaching of]</sup> Moses, <sup>[even]</sup> telling them not to circumcise their children and not to practice the <sup>[other]</sup> customs <sup>[of the Jewish religion]</sup>.
What do you think will happen when they hear that you have come <sup>[here to Jerusalem]</sup>?
So, here is what we would like you to do: We have four men who have made a vow <sup>[i.e., a special promise to God]</sup>.

Take these men and observe the ceremony of purification with them, pay their expenses so they can shave their heads <sup>[i.e., to signify that they are making a vow]</sup> and <sup>[then]</sup> everyone will know that there is no truth in what people have been told about you, but that you, too, are living properly and observing the Law of Moses.

But concerning Gentile believers, we wrote <sup>[to them]</sup> our decision, that they should avoid <sup>[eating]</sup> things sacrificed to idols, from <sup>[drinking]</sup> blood, from <sup>[eating]</sup> what is strangled and from sexual immorality.”

Then Paul took the men and the next day he observed the ceremony of purification with them. Then he went into the Temple, declaring <sup>[to the priest]</sup> when he would fulfill the <sup>[required]</sup> days of the purification <sup>[ceremony]</sup> and <sup>[be ready]</sup> for the sacrifices to be offered for each of them.
When the seven days <sup>[required by the vow]</sup> were almost completed, the Jews from Asia saw Paul in the Temple. They incited the whole crowd and took hold of him,

shouting, “<sup>[You]</sup> Israelites, help <sup>[us]</sup>! This man is teaching people everywhere <sup>[to be]</sup> against our people <sup>[i.e., the Jews]</sup>, the Law of Moses and this place <sup>[i.e., the Temple]</sup>; and in addition to that he has also brought Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup> into the Temple and has <sup>[thereby]</sup> contaminated this holy place.”
(For the Jews had previously seen him on a city street with Trophimus, the Ephesian, whom they assumed Paul had taken into the Temple <sup>[with him]</sup>).

<sup>[So]</sup>, the entire city was stirred up, and the mob rushed together and attacked Paul, dragging him out of the Temple. Immediately the <sup>[Temple]</sup> doors were closed <sup>[i.e., to secure it from further intrusion]</sup>.
And as they were attempting to kill Paul, the commander of the military unit was informed that the entire city of Jerusalem was rioting.
So, immediately he rushed down on the people with his soldiers and officers, and when the mob saw the commander and his soldiers <sup>[coming]</sup>, they stopped beating Paul.
Then the commanding officer approached Paul, took hold of him and ordered him to be secured with two chains. He then questioned him as to who he was and what he had done.
From the crowd, some shouted one thing and some another. When the commander could not hear what was being said because of the noise, he ordered Paul to be taken into the battalion headquarters.
When they got to the stairway <sup>[of the headquarters building]</sup> the soldiers had to carry Paul because of the <sup>[unrestrained]</sup> violence of the mob,
for the crowds that followed were <sup>[pressing in on them]</sup> shouting, “Kill him.”
As Paul was being brought into the headquarters building, he said to the commander, “Can I speak with you?” The commander replied <sup>[with surprise]</sup>, “You know the Greek language?
Are you not the Egyptian who, awhile back, stirred up a revolt and led four thousand of the ‘Dagger Men’ out into the wilderness?”
Paul answered, “<sup>[No]</sup>, I am a Jew from Tarsus, in Cilicia, a citizen of an important city, and I beg you to allow me to speak to these people.”

And when the commander gave him permission, Paul stood on the stairway and motioned to the people with his hand <sup>[for attention]</sup>. When they completely quieted down, he spoke to them in the Hebrew <sup>[i.e., Aramaic]</sup> language, saying,
“Brothers and fathers, listen to the defense I want to make to you.”
And when they heard him speaking in the Hebrew language, they listened all the more quietly. So, he continued,

“I am a Jew, born in Tarsus, in Cilicia, but raised <sup>[here]</sup> in this city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup>. <sup>[I was]</sup> instructed by Gamaliel <sup>[i.e., a renowned Jewish rabbi of the time]</sup> according to the strictest methods of observing the law of our forefathers. <sup>[I was very]</sup> eager to serve God, just as all of you are here today.
And I persecuted this ‘Way’ <sup>[even to the point of]</sup> putting people to death. <sup>[I had]</sup> both men and women bound <sup>[in chains]</sup> and locked up in prisons.

The head priest can also verify all this, and so can the body of <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders. They furnished me with letters to our fellow-Jews in Damascus, to which I also traveled to bring people back here to Jerusalem, bound in chains to be punished.
“And then it suddenly happened, about noon, as I was traveling and got close to Damascus, that this brilliant light from the sky flashed all around me.
I fell to the ground and heard a voice saying to me, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?’
I answered, ‘Who are you, sir?’ And the voice answered me, ‘I am Jesus from Nazareth, the One you are persecuting.’
Those who accompanied me saw the light clearly, but did not hear <sup>[i.e., understand]</sup> the voice of the one speaking to me.
Then I asked, ‘What am I to do, Lord?’ And the Lord replied to me, ‘Get up, go into Damascus and there you will be told everything <sup>[I have]</sup> appointed for you to do.’
And since I could not see <sup>[anything]</sup> because of the brilliance of the light, I had to be led by the hand of those with me into Damascus.
“<sup>[Then]</sup> Ananias, a man who was devoted to <sup>[observing]</sup> the Law of Moses and who had a good reputation among all the Jews who lived there,
came and stood beside me and said, ‘Brother Saul, regain your sight.’ And at that very moment I looked at him <sup>[fully able to see again]</sup>.

And he said <sup>[to me]</sup>, ‘The God of our forefathers has appointed you to know His will and to see the Righteous One <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>, and to hear His voice <sup>[speaking to you]</sup>.
For you will be His witness to all people of what you have seen and heard.
So now, what are you waiting for? Get up and be immersed <sup>[into Christ]</sup>, and have your sins washed away as you appeal to His name <sup>[i.e., to Christ's authority]</sup>.’
“And it happened that when I returned to Jerusalem and was praying in the Temple, I fell into a trance <sup>[i.e., a semi-conscious vision-like state]</sup>.
And I saw <sup>[and heard]</sup> the Lord saying to me, ‘Hurry up, get out of Jerusalem quickly, because the people <sup>[here]</sup> will reject your testimony about me.’
And I replied, ‘Lord, they know very well that I am <sup>[the one]</sup> who imprisoned and beat the believers <sup>[who assembled]</sup> in every synagogue,
and when your witness Stephen was killed, I stood there, approving <sup>[of it]</sup> and <sup>[even]</sup> guarding the clothing of those who stoned him to death.’
Then the Lord said to me, ‘Go, for I will send you far away from here to the Gentiles.’”

The people had listened to Paul speaking up to this point <sup>[i.e., until he said the word “Gentiles”]</sup>, then they raised their voices and shouted, “Do away with such a person from the earth; it is not right for him to live.”
And while the people shouted, waved around <sup>[torn]</sup> clothing and threw dust into the air,
the commander ordered Paul to be brought to headquarters and requested that he be interrogated by means of a flogging, in order to learn the reason why people were shouting against him.

Just as they were tying him up <sup>[in preparation]</sup> for the flogging, Paul said to the officer nearby, “Is it lawful for you to whip a Roman <sup>[citizen]</sup> before he has been <sup>[legally]</sup> condemned?”
When the officer heard this, he <sup>[immediately]</sup> went and told the commander, saying, “What are you going to do? For this man is a Roman citizen.”
Then the commander came and said to him, “Tell me, are you <sup>[really]</sup> a Roman citizen?” Paul said, “Yes.”
The commander answered, “It cost me a large sum of money to obtain this <sup>[right of]</sup> citizenship.” Paul replied, “But I am a Roman citizen by birth.”

Then those who were ready to interrogate Paul <sup>[with whips]</sup> immediately left him, and the commander also became fearful when he realized that he had chained a Roman citizen <sup>[illegally]</sup>.

But the next day the commander released Paul <sup>[from the chains]</sup> because he wanted to know what specific charges the Jews had against him. So, he ordered the leading priests and the entire <sup>[Jewish]</sup> Council to assemble, then brought Paul down and placed him in front of them.

Paul <sup>[then]</sup> looked intently at the Council <sup>[i.e., the Jewish Supreme Court called the “Sanhedrin”]</sup> and spoke <sup>[in his defense]</sup>: “Brothers, I have lived before God with a good conscience all my life.”
<sup>[Upon hearing this]</sup>, the head priest Ananias ordered that those standing closest to Paul hit him on the mouth.

Then Paul said to the head priest, “God will hit you, you white-washed wall <sup>[i.e., you hypocrite]</sup>. Are you sitting in judgment over me according to the Law of Moses and <sup>[yet]</sup> do you order me to be hit contrary to that law?”
Those who stood nearby replied, “Are you insulting God's head priest?”
Paul said, “Brothers, I did not know that he was the head priest, for it is written <sup>[Ex. 22:28]</sup>, ‘You shall not speak evil about a leader of your people.’”

When Paul realized that part <sup>[of the Sanhedrin]</sup> were Sadducees and the other part were Pharisees, he lifted up his voice before the Council and said, “Brothers, I am a Pharisee and the son of a Pharisee. And it is concerning our hope that the dead will be raised that I have been brought to trial.”
When he said this it stirred up a dispute between the Pharisees and Sadducees and the assembly became divided.
For the Sadducees believe there is no resurrection, angels or spirits, but the Pharisees accept all of them <sup>[to be true]</sup>.

A loud commotion developed <sup>[in the Council meeting]</sup>. Some of the teachers of the Law of Moses who belonged to the Pharisee party stood up and argued, saying, “We can find nothing wrong with this man. What if an angel or spirit did speak to him?”

And when a serious debate broke out, the commander was afraid that Paul might <sup>[virtually]</sup> be torn apart by the mob, so he ordered his soldiers to go down <sup>[to the Council meeting]</sup> and forcibly remove Paul and take him to the battalion headquarters.
The next night the Lord stood beside Paul <sup>[in a supernatural dream]</sup> and said, “Take courage, for just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also be my witness in Rome.”
When daylight came, <sup>[a group of]</sup> Jews conspired together, agreeing under oath that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.
There were more than forty men who made this pact.
They went to the leading priests and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and told them, “We have bound ourselves under an oath <sup>[with serious consequences]</sup> to eat nothing until we have killed Paul.

Therefore, <sup>[all of]</sup> you, together with the Jewish Council, should notify the commander so that he will bring Paul <sup>[back]</sup> down to you, as though you wanted to consider his case further. Then we will be ready to kill him, <sup>[even]</sup> before he comes near <sup>[you]</sup>.”
But Paul’s nephew learned of their plot and went to the headquarters and told Paul <sup>[all about it]</sup>.
So, Paul called for one of the officers and said <sup>[to him]</sup>, “Take this young man to the commander; he has something <sup>[important]</sup> to tell him.”
So, the officer took Paul’s nephew to the commander and said, “Paul, the prisoner, called for me and asked me to bring this young man to you. He has something to tell you.”
The commander took him by the hand, and walking along together, asked him privately, “What is it that you have to tell me?”
And the lad said, “The Jews have plotted to ask you to take Paul down to the Council <sup>[meeting]</sup> tomorrow, as though you were going to question him further.

But, do not listen to them, because over forty men have laid a plot and bound themselves under an oath <sup>[with serious consequences]</sup> neither to eat nor drink <sup>[anything]</sup> until they have killed him. They are now ready <sup>[to carry out the plot]</sup> and are just waiting for you to agree <sup>[to their arrangement]</sup>.”
So, the commander let the young man go, urging him, “Do not tell anyone that you have reported this to me.”

Then he called two of his officers and said, “Get two hundred soldiers ready to go to Caesarea, along with seventy horsemen and two hundred spearmen, by nine o’clock tonight.” <sup>[Note: This was calculated according to Jewish time]</sup>.
And he <sup>[also]</sup> requested that they furnish animals for Paul to ride so he could be brought safely to Felix, the governor.
The commander then wrote a letter like this:
“Claudius Lysias, <sup>[commander of the battalion, is writing]</sup> to His Excellency, Governor Felix. Greetings:
This man <sup>[i.e., Paul]</sup> was grabbed by the Jews and almost killed by them when I discovered what they were doing and rescued him, after learning that he was a Roman citizen.
So, I brought him before the Council because I wanted to know the reasons for the charges <sup>[they were]</sup> bringing against him.
I found out that he was being accused over questions regarding their law, but that they had no charges against him deserving of the death penalty, or <sup>[even]</sup> of being kept in prison.
And when I was told that there was a plot against the man, I sent him to you immediately, also ordering his accusers to present their case against him in front of you.”
So, the soldiers took charge of Paul and took him at night to Antipatris <sup>[Note: This was a town on the road between Jerusalem and Caesarea]</sup> as they were ordered.
The next day the soldiers allowed the horsemen to go on with Paul, while they <sup>[themselves]</sup> returned to headquarters.
When they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they presented Paul before him.
And when the governor read the letter, he asked what province Paul was from. When he learned that he was from Cilicia,
he said, “I will hear your case fully when your accusers also get here.” Then he ordered that Paul be kept <sup>[under guard]</sup> in Herod's palace.

Five days later the head priest, Ananias, came down <sup>[to Caesarea]</sup> with certain <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and an attorney <sup>[named]</sup> Tertullus, who brought their case against Paul before the governor.

When Paul was called <sup>[to appear before the court]</sup> Tertullus began the prosecution by saying, “Your Excellency Felix: Since through you we are able to enjoy peace <sup>[in this province]</sup> and under your jurisdiction the problems in our society are being <sup>[properly]</sup> dealt with,
we welcome all such efforts in each district with full gratitude.
But, not wanting to prolong matters for you, I urge you to give consideration to this brief account <sup>[of the problem we face]</sup>.
We have found this man <sup>[i.e., Paul]</sup> to be extremely bothersome, and an instigator of strife among the Jews throughout the world and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
We arrested him, for he attempted to desecrate <sup>[even]</sup> the Temple
Some ancient manuscripts add this verse and we would have judged him according to our law but commander Lysias came and forcibly took him out of our hands
<sup>[and]</sup> you will be able to determine the nature of our accusations against him by questioning him yourself.”
Then the Jews also joined in <sup>[making]</sup> the charge, and confirmed all the facts that were being presented.

And when the governor had motioned for him to speak, Paul answered <sup>[the charges, by saying]</sup>, “I know <sup>[Your Excellency]</sup> that you have administered justice for this nation for many years, so I am glad to make my defense <sup>[before you]</sup>.
<sup>[I know]</sup> you can verify that it has not been over twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
<sup>[While there]</sup> I was not observed arguing with anyone or trying to stir up a crowd <sup>[to riot]</sup> in the Temple or in the synagogues or <sup>[anywhere else]</sup> in the city.
Neither can anyone prove to you the charges they are now bringing against me.

But, I declare to you, I am serving the God of our forefathers according to the ‘Way,’ called <sup>[by my detractors]</sup> a sect, and I believe everything that is in harmony with the Law of Moses and what is written in <sup>[the writings of]</sup> the prophets.
I have a hope in God that there will be a resurrection both for those who have done right and those who have done wrong, just as the Jews themselves also expect.
So, for this reason I make every effort to have a clear conscience before God and men at all times.
“Now after several years I came <sup>[to Jerusalem]</sup> to bring relief for the poor people of my nation and to present my offerings.

While doing this, they <sup>[i.e., the Jews]</sup> found out that I had undergone the rites of purification in the Temple. There was no crowd and no commotion. But certain Jews from <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia
should have been here before you, <sup>[Your Excellency]</sup> to make their accusations, if they <sup>[really]</sup> had a valid charge against me.
Or let these men themselves <sup>[i.e., members of the Sanhedrin]</sup> testify what they found wrong with me when I stood before their Council <sup>[meeting]</sup>.

However, this one point, which I made when I stood among them <sup>[could have been objectionable]</sup>: I shouted, ‘The reason I am being charged before you today is <sup>[my preaching of]</sup> the resurrection of the dead.’”

But because Felix <sup>[felt he]</sup> had more accurate knowledge of the “Way” <sup>[than was being presented by Paul’s accusers]</sup>, he dismissed them, saying, “When commander Lysias comes down <sup>[to Caesarea]</sup> I will make a decision on your case.”
So, he ordered his officer to keep Paul in custody, yet with some freedom, and not to prevent any of his friends from ministering to his needs.

After a number of days, Felix and Drusilla, his wife, who was a Jewess, came <sup>[back to the palace]</sup> and sent for Paul to <sup>[come and]</sup> tell them about <sup>[the]</sup> faith in Christ Jesus.

And as he discussed the matters of doing right, and <sup>[maintaining]</sup> self control and the coming judgment, Felix became terrified and answered, “Go on away for now; I will call you back when it is convenient for me.”
He had hoped, along with everything else, that he would receive money from Paul, so he sent for him more frequently and had discussions with him.
So, Felix allowed Paul to remain in chains, because he wanted to gain favor with the Jews <sup>[during that period of time]</sup>. Two years passed and Felix was succeeded by Portius Festus.
Three days after Festus came to the province he went from Caesarea up to Jerusalem.
The leading priests and the leading Jewish men <sup>[there]</sup> informed Festus of the charges against Paul. Then they begged him
to do them a favor by having Paul brought <sup>[from Caesarea]</sup> to Jerusalem, for they were plotting to kill him on the way.

Festus answered that <sup>[since]</sup> Paul was being held in custody in Caesarea, and <sup>[since]</sup> he himself was soon going there <sup>[i.e., he could see Paul at Caesarea at that time]</sup>.
So, he said, “Let your leaders go down with me and if there is anything <sup>[found]</sup> wrong with the man, let them bring their charges against him <sup>[at that time]</sup>.”

After staying not more than eight or ten <sup>[more]</sup> days <sup>[there in Jerusalem]</sup>, Festus went down to Caesarea and the following day he ordered Paul to appear before him as he sat in his court of justice.
When Paul appeared, the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> leaders who had accompanied Festus down from Jerusalem gathered around him and brought many serious charges against him, which they were not able to prove.
Paul replied in his defense, “I have not committed any sin against the law of the Jews, nor against the Temple, nor against Caesar.”
But Festus, wanting to gain favor with the Jews <sup>[See 24:27]</sup>, answered Paul, “Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and stand trial before me there?”
Paul replied, “I am <sup>[already]</sup> standing before a court of Caesar's authority, where I deserve to be tried. I have done nothing wrong to the Jews, as you very well know.

If then I am a criminal and have committed any crime that deserves the death penalty, I will not try to avoid being put to death. But if none of the charges I have been accused of are true, <sup>[then]</sup> no one has the right to turn me over <sup>[to the authorities]</sup>. I make my appeal to Caesar.”
After conferring with his advisors, Festus answered Paul, “<sup>[Since]</sup> you have appealed to Caesar, then you will go to Caesar.”

After a number of days King Agrippa <sup>[Note: This man was the great grandson of Herod the Great. See Matt. 2:1]</sup> and <sup>[his wife]</sup> Bernice arrived at Caesarea and came to greet Festus.
While they stayed there <sup>[with him]</sup> for many days, Festus discussed Paul's case with the king, saying, “There is this man <sup>[named Paul]</sup> who was left in custody by Felix.
When I was at Jerusalem, the leading priests and the Jewish elders presented his case before me to make a judgment against him.

I answered them that it is not the custom of the Romans to turn over any person <sup>[for sentencing]</sup> before he is faced by his accusers and has had the opportunity to defend himself against the charges made against him.

When therefore his accusers had assembled here I did not delay <sup>[in dealing with the matter; in fact]</sup> the very next day I sat in court and summoned the man to be brought <sup>[before me]</sup>.
When his accusers stood up, they brought no charge of wrongdoing as I had expected <sup>[they would]</sup>.
All they had was a controversy with him over their own religion and about someone named Jesus, who had died <sup>[but]</sup> whom Paul alleges is <sup>[now]</sup> alive.
And I was perplexed as to how to proceed with the investigation, so I <sup>[finally]</sup> asked him if he would go to Jerusalem to have his case judged there.
But when Paul requested that his case be reviewed for a decision by Caesar, I ordered him to remain in custody until I could send him to Caesar.”
Agrippa said to Festus, “I had been wanting to hear this man's testimony myself.” <sup>[Festus replied]</sup>, “Tomorrow you will <sup>[have the opportunity to]</sup> hear him.”
So, the next day, when Agrippa and <sup>[his wife]</sup> Bernice, arrived in an elaborate procession, they entered the hearing chamber, along with the commanders and leading men of the city.

Then Festus spoke, “King Agrippa, and all you gentlemen assembled with us, you see this man whose case a large number of Jews, <sup>[first]</sup> at Jerusalem and <sup>[then]</sup> here <sup>[in Caesarea]</sup> have petitioned me <sup>[to resolve]</sup>. They have been clamoring for him to be put to death,

but I found that he had committed nothing which deserved the death <sup>[penalty]</sup>. And since he himself appealed <sup>[his case]</sup> to the Emperor, I decided to send him <sup>[to Rome]</sup>.

But I do not have anything specific to write to His Majesty. This is the reason I have brought him before this assembly, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after you have questioned him, I would <sup>[then]</sup> have something <sup>[substantial]</sup> to write.
For it does not seem reasonable for me to send a prisoner <sup>[to Rome]</sup> without indicating what the <sup>[formal]</sup> charges are against him.”
Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” So, Paul motioned <sup>[to the assembly]</sup> with his hand <sup>[for attention]</sup> and began speaking in his defense:
“King Agrippa, I consider myself fortunate that I am able to defend myself before you concerning all the charges being brought against me by the Jews,
especially since you are an expert on all the customs and issues discussed among the Jews. So, for this reason I request that you hear <sup>[my response to them]</sup> patiently.
All the Jews know how I lived during my youth and my early days among <sup>[the people of]</sup> my nation, and <sup>[especially here]</sup> in Jerusalem.
They know of my early background; if they were willing to, they could testify that I lived as a <sup>[faithful]</sup> Pharisee, <sup>[which is]</sup> the strictest sect of our religion.
And now I am standing <sup>[here]</sup>, being judged because <sup>[I believe that]</sup> God will fulfill the hope He promised to our forefathers.

This is the promise our twelve tribes <sup>[i.e., family groups]</sup>, who earnestly serve God night and day, hope to see fulfilled. It is because <sup>[I advocate]</sup> this hope, O King, that I am being charged <sup>[with wrongdoing]</sup> by the Jews.
So, why should you <sup>[Jews]</sup> consider it incredible for God to raise the dead?
I truly thought within myself <sup>[that it was right]</sup> to do many things which were opposed to the name of Jesus from Nazareth.

So, I was doing this in Jerusalem, having received <sup>[my]</sup> authority from the leading priests. I both imprisoned many of the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup> and <sup>[even]</sup> voiced my approval when they were put to death <sup>[See 8:1]</sup>.

I often persecuted them in the synagogues, <sup>[even]</sup> trying to get them to blaspheme <sup>[i.e., speak against God]</sup>. I had extreme anger toward them and <sup>[even]</sup> traveled to foreign cities <sup>[in my effort]</sup> to persecute them.
“While on a journey to Damascus for this purpose, I was acting under the authority and directive of the leading priests.
<sup>[Then]</sup> along the road, about noon, O King, I saw a light from the sky, brighter than the sun, shining around me and those traveling with me.

When we all fell to the ground I heard a voice speaking in the Hebrew <sup>[i.e., Aramaic]</sup> language. <sup>[It said]</sup>, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? You are only hurting yourself by kicking against the spurs <sup>[i.e., opposing the work of God]</sup>.’
I answered, ‘Who are you, sir?’ And the Lord said, ‘I am Jesus, the One you are persecuting.

But, get up onto your feet, for the reason I have appeared to you is to appoint you a minister and witness of both the things you have <sup>[already]</sup> seen of me and the things I will <sup>[reveal when]</sup> appearing to you.
I will rescue you from the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people and from the Gentiles. I am sending you to them,

to open their eyes <sup>[to the truth]</sup> so they will turn from darkness to light and from the power <sup>[i.e., the influence]</sup> of Satan to God. <sup>[Then]</sup> they can receive the forgiveness of sins and the inheritance <sup>[of never ending life]</sup>, along with <sup>[all]</sup> those who are set apart <sup>[for God]</sup> by trusting in Me.’
“As a result of this, O King, I did not disobey <sup>[the mandate of]</sup> this vision from heaven

but, <sup>[instead]</sup> declared to the people of Damascus first, <sup>[then]</sup> to the people of Jerusalem, and <sup>[finally to those]</sup> throughout all of the district of Judea, as well as to the Gentiles <sup>[everywhere]</sup> that they should repent <sup>[i.e., change their hearts and lives]</sup> and turn to God, doing deeds which demonstrate their repentance.
For this reason the Jews grabbed me in the Temple and attempted to kill me.

But I obtained help from God and so <sup>[I am able to]</sup> stand here to this day, testifying to both the common people and to those in prominence. <sup>[I am]</sup> saying only what the prophets and Moses said would happen,

namely, that the Christ must suffer and be the first to rise from the dead <sup>[i.e., never to die again]</sup> and then proclaim the light <sup>[of the Gospel]</sup> to the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people and to the Gentiles.”
As Paul made his defense in this way, Festus said to him loudly, “Paul, you are crazy. All your education is driving you insane.”
But Paul replied, “I am not crazy, Your Excellency Festus; I am <sup>[just]</sup> speaking a message of truth and sound reason.

I can speak boldly of these things because the King knows <sup>[they are true]</sup>. And I am convinced that none of these things are hidden from you, for none of them have been done in a corner <sup>[i.e., secretly]</sup>.
King Agrippa, do you believe <sup>[the message of]</sup> the prophets? <sup>[Yes]</sup> I know you do.”
Agrippa said to Paul, “You expect to persuade me to be a Christian with such little effort <sup>[or, in such a short time]</sup>?”

Paul replied, “I wish to God that, whether it would take a little or a lot, not only you, but all those who hear me today, might become <sup>[Christians]</sup> like me, except <sup>[not]</sup> with these chains.”
Then King Agrippa, <sup>[his wife]</sup> Bernice, Governor <sup>[Festus]</sup> and <sup>[the commanders and leading men of the city, 25:23]</sup> all rose up,
and when they left <sup>[the room]</sup> they conferred with one another, saying, “This man is not doing anything deserving of the death penalty or <sup>[even]</sup> imprisonment.”
Then Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been released if he had not appealed <sup>[his case]</sup> to Caesar.”
And when it was decided that we should sail for Italy, they transferred Paul and certain other prisoners to the custody of a military officer named Julius, of the Augustan battalion.

We boarded a ship <sup>[originating]</sup> from Adramyttium which was ready to sail <sup>[from here in Caesarea]</sup>, heading out to sea for parts of the coast of <sup>[the province of]</sup> Asia. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, went with us.

The next day we stopped at Sidon <sup>[i.e., a seaport on the northwest coast of Palestine]</sup>. Julius treated Paul with kindness, <sup>[even]</sup> giving him the opportunity to meet his friends <sup>[there]</sup> and receive help <sup>[from them]</sup>.
We headed out to sea from there and sailed along the sheltered side of Cyprus because the wind was against us.
When we had sailed across the sea, off <sup>[the coast of]</sup> Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra, <sup>[a town]</sup> of Lycia.
There Julius, the military officer, arranged <sup>[passage]</sup> for us on a ship, <sup>[originating]</sup> from Alexandria, <sup>[and]</sup> bound for Italy, and put us on board.
When we had sailed slowly for many days <sup>[and]</sup> had trouble passing Cnidus because of an unfavorable wind, we sailed on the sheltered side of Crete and on past Salmone.

After making our way along the coast <sup>[of Crete]</sup> with difficulty, we arrived at a place called Fair Harbors <sup>[i.e., the principal seaport of the island]</sup>, which was near the town of Lasea.

After much time had passed, the voyage became more dangerous because it was <sup>[now]</sup> past the Day of Atonement <sup>[Note: This would have been around September or October, when a sea voyage involved rough sailing]</sup>. So, Paul began warning the people <sup>[aboard ship]</sup>,
saying, “Sirs, I can see that this voyage will result in suffering and much loss, not only to the ship and its cargo, but also to our <sup>[very]</sup> lives.”
But the military officer paid more attention to the captain and to the owner of the ship than to what Paul was saying.

And, because the harbor was not suitable for staying in all winter, most of those on board were in favor of putting out to sea from there. They were hoping to reach Phoenix, <sup>[another]</sup> harbor on <sup>[the island of]</sup> Crete, which faced northwest and southwest, and <sup>[then]</sup> to spend the winter there.
When the south wind began to blow gently, they raised anchor, thinking this was the opportunity they had hoped for, <sup>[then]</sup> sailed close to the coastline of <sup>[southern]</sup> Crete.
But a short time later, a violent northeasterly wind they called “Euraquilo” swept down,
and when the ship was caught <sup>[by it]</sup>, we were not able to face the wind, so had to give in and allow the ship to drift.
Then, sailing on the sheltered side of a small island named Cauda, we experienced difficulty in trying to secure the ship’s life-boat.

And when they <sup>[finally]</sup> got the boat hoisted up, they slung <sup>[rope]</sup> cables underneath <sup>[and around]</sup> the hull <sup>[of the ship to reinforce it]</sup>. Then, fearing the ship would run aground on the <sup>[shifting]</sup>, shallow sandbar <sup>[called]</sup> Syrtis, they lowered their <sup>[navigation]</sup> gear <sup>[Note: This may have been sails, rigging, etc.]</sup> and so were driven <sup>[as a derelict by the wind]</sup>.
As we were being severely battered by the storm, they began the next day to throw the cargo overboard <sup>[i.e., to lighten the ship]</sup>.
On the third day, they handed <sup>[the rest of]</sup> the ship’s gear to each other, and threw it overboard <sup>[Note: This was perhaps furniture, rigging, sails, baggage, etc.]</sup>.
When they could see neither the sun nor the stars for many days <sup>[due to the storm]</sup>, and with the wind blowing furiously on them, they gave up all hope of <sup>[ever]</sup> being saved.

When they had gone without food for a long time, Paul stood in the middle of the crew and spoke, “Men, you should have listened to me and not sailed from Crete and thereby have to experience such suffering and loss.
But, now I want to encourage you to cheer up, for there will be no loss of life among you, but only the ship <sup>[will be lost]</sup>.
For an angel from God, to whom I belong and whom I serve, appeared to me last night,
saying, ‘Do not be afraid Paul, for you must appear before Caesar, and look, God will grant you <sup>[the safety of]</sup> all these men aboard ship <sup>[along]</sup> with yourself.’
So men, cheer up, for I believe God, that everything will turn out just as I was told it would.
But we must be washed onto the shore of a certain island.”
When the fourteenth night came, and while we were being tossed around <sup>[helplessly]</sup> in the Adriatic Sea, about midnight the crew suspected they were nearing land.
So, they tested for depth and found <sup>[they were in]</sup> 120 feet <sup>[of water]</sup>. Then a little later they tested again and found it to be 90 feet.
So, fearing the possibility of being run aground on a rocky shore, they dropped four anchors from the stern and longed for daylight <sup>[to come]</sup>.
The crew had <sup>[by now]</sup> lowered the life-boat into the water, pretending to be dropping anchors from the bow, <sup>[but were in reality]</sup> attempting to abandon ship.
Paul said to the military officer <sup>[Julius]</sup> and to his soldiers, “Unless these men stay aboard the ship, none of you will be saved.”
So, the soldiers cut the ropes securing the life-boat and let it drift away.

As it was dawning, Paul urged them <sup>[i.e., the crew, soldiers and probably the passengers]</sup> to eat something, saying, “You have been waiting for fourteen days <sup>[for the weather to break]</sup> and you have continued to fast <sup>[all that time]</sup>, eating nothing <sup>[at all]</sup>.
I urge you to eat some food; it will be for your own good, for not one hair from anyone's head will be lost.”
And when he had said this he took bread, gave thanks to God for it in front of everyone, then broke it and began to eat.
Then they were all encouraged and they themselves took something to eat also.
(The total number of us aboard the ship was 276 persons).
And when they had eaten sufficiently, they began lightening the ship <sup>[so it would float higher]</sup> by throwing their wheat overboard.

When it got daylight they saw an island they did not recognize, but noticed <sup>[it had]</sup> a particular bay with a <sup>[suitable]</sup> beach. So, they discussed whether it would be possible to run the ship aground on the beach.

<sup>[Finally]</sup> they cut the ropes, dropping the anchors into the water and at the same time they released the oars used for steering. Then they hoisted the bow-sail to the wind and headed <sup>[straight]</sup> for the beach.
Landing where two <sup>[strong]</sup> currents met, the ship ran aground, its bow lodging <sup>[in the sand]</sup> while its stern began to break up from the driving surf.
<sup>[Meanwhile]</sup> the soldiers had decided to kill the prisoners so that none of them would swim away and escape.

But the military officer, wanting to save Paul <sup>[from being killed]</sup>, prevented them from doing this. <sup>[Instead]</sup> he ordered those who could swim to jump overboard and be the first to reach land.
The rest <sup>[he reasoned]</sup> could reach shore by floating on planks or other debris from the ship. And so it happened that all of them escaped safely to land.
After we escaped <sup>[from the sinking ship]</sup> we became aware that the island <sup>[on which we had landed]</sup> was Melita <sup>[i.e., present-day Malta]</sup>.
The natives <sup>[there]</sup> showed us uncommon kindness. It had begun to rain and was <sup>[getting]</sup> cold, so they built a fire and made us feel welcome.
But as Paul gathered a bundle of sticks, and was placing them on the fire, a snake crawled out because of the heat and clung to his hand.

And when the natives saw the poisonous creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “This man must be a murderer because, even though he escaped from the sea, <sup>[divine]</sup> justice is not going to allow him to live.”
However, Paul shook off the creature into the fire without suffering any harm.

But the natives expected him to swell up <sup>[from snake bite]</sup> or fall dead suddenly, but after expecting this for a long time and not seeing any harm come to him, they changed their minds and said he was a god.
Now in that general vicinity there was property belonging to the chief of the island, a man named Publius, who welcomed us courteously and entertained us for three days.
And it happened that Publius’ father lay sick with a fever and dysentery. Paul went <sup>[to his house]</sup> and, after praying and placing hands on him, healed him.
When he had done this, the rest of those on the island who had diseases, <sup>[also]</sup> came and were healed.
These people also showered many honors on us and when we sailed <sup>[away]</sup>, they brought the provisions we needed and put them aboard <sup>[the ship]</sup>.

After three months we set sail on a ship that had spent the winter on the island <sup>[of Melita]</sup>. This ship had originated from Alexandria and was designated as “Twin Brothers” <sup>[Note: The ship may have been named this because of its prow containing the figures of the mythical twin gods of sailors, Castor and Pollux]</sup>.
And docking at Syracuse <sup>[i.e., a town on the eastern shore of Sicily]</sup>, we stayed there three days.

From there we sailed around <sup>[and then north]</sup> until we arrived at Rhegium <sup>[i.e., a town on the “toe” of Italy]</sup>. The next day a south wind began blowing, and on the second day we arrived at Puteoli <sup>[i.e., a town about half-way to Rome]</sup>,
where we found <sup>[some Christian]</sup> brothers. They urged us to stay with them for seven days. So, <sup>[that is how]</sup> we finally got to Rome.

<sup>[So]</sup>, hearing that we were coming, the brothers <sup>[from Rome]</sup> traveled from there as far <sup>[south]</sup> as “The Market of Appius” and “The Three Inns” to meet us. When Paul saw them he thanked God and was <sup>[very]</sup> encouraged.
When we entered Rome <sup>[itself]</sup> Paul was permitted to live alone except for a soldier who guarded him.

And it happened after three days that Paul called together those who were the leading Jews, and when they assembled he said to them, “Brothers, although I had done nothing against the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people or the customs of our forefathers, yet I was made a prisoner at Jerusalem, being placed in the custody of the Romans <sup>[while there]</sup>.
After questioning me, they wanted to release me because they could not find any grounds for sentencing me to death.
But when the Jews objected to this, I was compelled to appeal <sup>[my case]</sup> to Caesar, even though I had no <sup>[legitimate]</sup> charge <sup>[to bring]</sup> against my country.
It was for this reason that I requested to see you and talk with you, for I am wearing this chain because of the hope held by the Israelites.”
Then they said to him, “We did not receive any letters from Judea about you, nor did any of the brothers come here to report anything bad about you.
But we would like to hear what you think, for according to our knowledge, this sect <sup>[you belong to]</sup> is being spoken against everywhere.”

And when they had arranged a day, large numbers of people came to Paul's house. He explained <sup>[everything to them]</sup>, testifying from morning until evening concerning the kingdom of God and concerning <sup>[the Messiahship of]</sup> Jesus, both from the Law of Moses and from <sup>[the writings of]</sup> the prophets.
And some people believed what Paul said and some refused to believe.

And when they could not agree among themselves they left, after hearing Paul make this one <sup>[important]</sup> statement: “The Holy Spirit has spoken truthfully to your forefathers through Isaiah the prophet,

saying, <sup>[Isa. 6:9-10]</sup>, ‘Go to these people and say, you will hear all right, but you will not understand <sup>[what you hear]</sup>. You will see all right, but you will not perceive <sup>[what you see]</sup>.

For these people’s minds are dull, and their ears are hard of hearing, and they have shut their eyes. For, if this were not the case, they would perceive what they see with their eyes, and understand what they hear with their ears, and comprehend with their minds, and would turn <sup>[back to God]</sup> again and I <sup>[God]</sup> would heal them <sup>[from their sins]</sup>.’
Then let it be known to you that salvation from God is <sup>[now]</sup> being announced to the Gentiles. They will listen <sup>[to it]</sup>.”
Some ancient manuscripts contain this verse And when he had said these words, the Jews left, disputing about the matter among themselves.
Then Paul lived for two whole years in his own rented house and welcomed everyone who visited him.
He preached to them about the kingdom of God and taught things about the Lord Jesus Christ, with no one preventing him.
